preFix.title: Making a simple leather notebook cover
preFix.subtitle: Cut and saddle-stitch a veg-tan leather cover for a pocket notebook
preFix.excerpt: How to make a leather cover for a pocket notebook: cutting and bevelling the leather panel, punching stitching holes, saddle-stitching the spine flap, and burnishing the edges.
preFix.sourceNotes: Al Stohlman, The Art of Hand Sewing Leather.

content.text: A hand-cut and saddle-stitched leather notebook cover uses vegetable-tanned leather at 1.0 to 1.5 mm thickness: firm enough to hold its shape. Thin enough to fold flat. The cover wraps around a standard A6 or pocket-sized notebook and is held closed with an elastic cord loop.
Step 1, cut and mark the leather

Measure the open notebook flat. Add 5 mm to the height and 10 mm to the overall width for a slight overhang. Score the cut lines into the grain side of the leather with the bone folder using a metal ruler. Cut along the scored line with the leatherwork knife and a cutting mat. A single, firm pass cuts cleanly; multiple light passes tear the fibres.
Step 2, bevel and burnish the edges

Run the edge beveller along all four edges of the cut panel on both the grain and flesh sides. Removing the sharp right-angle corners. Wet the bevelled edges lightly with water. Rub the leather burnisher back and forth along each edge until the fibres compress into a smooth. Rounded bead. The burnishing process takes 30 to 60 seconds per edge and produces a hard, polished finish.
Step 3, punch the stitch line and add the elastic cord

Mark the spine fold lines with the bone folder. Pierce a stitch line along the spine fold using a stitching pricking iron or a single-prong punch at 3 mm spacing. Cut a slot near the centre of the back cover for the elastic cord closure loop. Thread the round elastic cord through. And tie a figure-eight knot on the flesh side to anchor it.

Cut a length of linen thread four times the length of the stitch run. Run it through the beeswax block to wax it. Thread a bookbinding needle at each end. Pass one needle through the first hole from the grain side to establish the starting knot. Saddle-stitch: pass the right needle through the next hole. Then the left needle through the same hole from the other side. Both needles must pass through each hole. At the last hole, backstitch two holes and trim the thread flush.

attrs.fix: Re-wet the edge with a damp fingertip before burnishing. The fibres must be moist to compact cleanly.
attrs.cause: The leather was not wet enough before burnishing, or the edge beveller cut too deep.
attrs.symptom: Edges tear or shred during burnishing

attrs.fix: Mark a groove line with the bone folder and ruler before punching all holes; the groove guides the punch.
attrs.cause: The stitching holes were not marked with a ruler before punching.

content.text: Step-by-step instructions for Making a simple leather notebook cover go here.
